narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background and plans to attend law school full-time, you've put significant effort into your application, and I appreciate how challenging this process can feel. The admissions model we use predicts your likelihood of failing the bar exam on the first try, and unfortunately, it estimated a 65% chance of failure for you, which crossed the threshold for rejecting your application to protect the school's outcomes. This decision hinged most heavily on your first-year school decile ranking, where you placed in the top decile—yet counterintuitively, this stood out as the strongest factor pushing the prediction toward failure compared to typical students who average around the middle of their class. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.30, well below the dataset average of 3.21, was the next biggest influence, reinforcing the model's concern about your bar exam readiness since many successful students have stronger GPAs. These two academic markers carried the most weight in the decision, outweighing other aspects of your profile. On a more positive note, your full-time enrollment plans nudged the prediction slightly toward passing, aligning with the vast majority of students (over 92%) who pursue full-time and tend to fare better. Similarly, your upper-middle family income provided a small boost toward success, as it's in line with backgrounds that often support stronger outcomes. Your third-year decile of 3 (above average) had a minor positive pull, while your LSAT score of 29 (below the typical 36.7) offered just a slight counterbalance but wasn't enough to shift the overall outlook. Compared to typical applicants who pass the bar, your early academic decile and GPA were notably weaker drivers toward risk, even with your solid family support and full-time commitment. This isn't a judgment on your potential—it's the model's data-driven assessment based on patterns from past students. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your undergraduate record through post-bac courses or relevant experience, and perhaps seeking mentorship from bar-pass alumni to address these key gaps. You're capable of turning this around, and many students in similar positions have succeeded by targeting these areas—keep pushing, and reapply stronger.
